articles.title: West Virginia home-price movement in the latest FHFA HPI
articles.dek: Quarterly, annual, and longer-run state HPI comparisons with a clear boundary between market movement and property value.
articles.previewText: Review the latest FHFA home-price movement for West Virginia and why an index cannot value a specific property.
articles.metaDescription: West Virginia home-price movement in the latest FHFA HPI. Review the latest FHFA home-price movement for West Virginia and why an index cannot value a specific.

sections.heading: The latest statewide index reading
body: West Virginia's latest FHFA purchase-only HPI is 334.6, accompanied by a quarterly change of 2.7%. For West Virginia, the 334.6 index level is a benchmark for repeat-sale price movement, not a dollar-denominated typical home price. A quarter in which the index rose by 2.7% can help a borrower date the statewide market context, but it cannot identify the value or asking price of a selected property. Reading 334.6 with 2.7% also keeps the level separate from the rate of change. Alongside the 334.6 state index, local inventory, property type, condition, concessions, taxes, insurance, and financing costs still need current evidence before the trend informs a decision.

sections.heading: Quarterly and annual windows tell different stories
body: Over the latest quarter, West Virginia's purchase-only HPI rose by 2.7%; over the latest year, it rose by 3.9%. The 2.7% figure is more sensitive to recent movement, while 3.9% compares the latest quarter with the matching quarter a year earlier. A borrower should not choose between the 2.7% and 3.9% windows based only on which looks more favorable, because the comparisons answer different timing questions. Together they show whether recent statewide movement is aligned with or different from the broader year without predicting the next quarter, a local bidding environment, or the price path of one home in West Virginia.

sections.heading: Place the latest movement in a longer sequence
body: Across the five-year comparison, the West Virginia HPI rose by 40.7%, compared with the latest annual movement of 3.9%. The 40.7% change places the current 334.6 index reading in a longer repeat-sale sequence, but it can span very different rate, inventory, and economic conditions. The 40.7% change should not be applied to an old purchase price to estimate a current property value. For a borrower, the contrast between 40.7% and 3.9% is most useful as a reminder that search assumptions can age at different speeds. Even when the 40.7% direction is clear, current comparable properties and a property-specific payment worksheet remain necessary.

sections.heading: Use the index to test local assumptions
body: The combination of a 2.7% quarterly change, 3.9% annual change, and 40.7% five-year change gives West Virginia borrowers three dated views of the same statewide index. If the 2.7%, 3.9%, and 40.7% windows point in different directions, the divergence shows that timing matters; if they point together, statewide consistency still does not erase local variation. A West Virginia search plan can use those three changes to ask whether current listings, seller concessions, and property types resemble the broad state movement. It should not use 334.6 as a sale price or use any of the percentage changes as a forecast, because FHFA HPI measures repeat-sale movement rather than the complete set of homes available to one borrower.

sections.heading: The HPI cannot value a property or set a payment
body: An HPI level of 334.6 does not establish a West Virginia property's dollar value, and an 3.9% annual movement does not establish how much any home gained or lost. The 334.6 index excludes the borrower's down payment, loan amount, interest rate, taxes, insurance, association costs, repairs, and time horizon. FHFA can also revise published values, so the 2.7% and 40.7% comparisons should retain their release period. The 334.6 index level must remain separate from the dollar figures used in a property review. Those limits make the 334.6 state index useful for orientation but unsuitable as an appraisal, offer recommendation, equity calculation, promised appreciation claim, payment quote, or underwriting conclusion.

sections.heading: Connect the state trend to current property evidence
body: A practical West Virginia borrower review can record 2.7%, 3.9%, and 40.7% as statewide trend markers, then compare them with current local listings and recent property-level evidence. For a purchase, keep the 2.7% state change as context while adding the selected price, inspection findings, appraisal process, taxes, insurance, cash-to-close, and loan disclosures. For a refinance or equity question, begin with a defensible property review rather than multiplying a prior value by 3.9% or 40.7%. This approach preserves the information in the 334.6 HPI while giving current local and borrower-specific facts the decision-making role that a statewide repeat-sale index cannot fill.

articles.methodology: This article uses the official FHFA purchase-only state quarterly HPI and calculates changes from matching prior-quarter, prior-year, and five-year index observations when available. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: FHFA may revise index values, statewide movement can mask local and property-type differences, and an index level is not a dollar-denominated home value. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: West Virginia statewide labor market: latest employment evidence
articles.dek: A borrower-focused reading of the latest BLS labor-force, employment, unemployment, and rate measures for West Virginia.
articles.previewText: See what changed in West Virginia's labor market and why broad employment data cannot determine mortgage eligibility.
articles.metaDescription: West Virginia statewide labor market: latest employment evidence. See what changed in West Virginia's labor market and why broad employment data cannot.

evidenceFacts.label: change in unemployment rate from the prior month
change in unemployment rate from a year earlier

sections.heading: The latest labor-force counts
body: The latest LAUS release reports a West Virginia labor force of 775,073, with 741,659 people employed and 33,414 unemployed under the program's definitions. Those three counts belong together: 741,659 plus 33,414 explains the scale behind the published 4.3% unemployment rate. For mortgage planning, the 775,073 state-level labor force describes the surrounding market rather than the income or employment record of an applicant. A borrower can use the 775,073 labor-force baseline to understand the size of the measured market, but qualification still depends on documented income, employment continuity, debts, assets, and the rules of the loan being considered.

sections.heading: What changed from the prior month
body: West Virginia's unemployment rate fell from 4.4% in the prior month to 4.3% in the latest month, a change of -0.1%. A -0.1% move can reflect current conditions, normal sampling movement, and later revisions, so it should be read with the underlying 33,414 unemployment count and 775,073 labor force. The -0.1% comparison does not show that a particular employer is hiring or cutting jobs, and it cannot predict the next release. For a West Virginia borrower, the monthly change is a prompt to date economic assumptions and verify personal employment documentation, not a reason to assume approval, denial, or a particular loan price.

sections.heading: The year-over-year comparison
body: Compared with 4.0% a year earlier, the latest West Virginia unemployment rate of 4.3% rose by 0.3%. The year-over-year window reduces the risk of interpreting only one monthly step, but the 0.3% difference still describes a population estimate rather than an individual's job stability. Reading 4.0%, 4.4%, and 4.3% together shows whether the latest month sits within a wider movement without forecasting what follows. A borrower should keep that broad West Virginia pattern separate from pay history, variable compensation, self-employment records, contracts, leave, job changes, and other facts a lender may need to document.

sections.heading: Why the rate needs its underlying counts
body: At 4.3%, West Virginia's unemployment rate summarizes 33,414 unemployed people within a 775,073 labor force; the rate should not be discussed without those counts. The 741,659 employment estimate is an economic measure and does not distinguish borrowers by hours, tenure, pay structure, industry, or the documentation available for a loan file. That makes 741,659 useful for local context but unsuitable as evidence that one household's income is stable or sufficient. Keeping the 4.3% rate attached to 775,073, 741,659, and 33,414 prevents a small percentage movement from carrying more borrower-level meaning than the LAUS release supports.

sections.heading: Labor statistics are not an income review
body: Neither 741,659 employed people nor a 4.3% unemployment rate tells a lender what one West Virginia applicant earns, whether that income is eligible, or whether it is likely to continue. The -0.1% monthly rate change also cannot substitute for pay stubs, tax returns, business records, contracts, benefit documentation, or an explanation of a recent job transition. The 4.3% rate can be relevant to timing and local economic questions while remaining outside the underwriting decision. That boundary protects borrowers from treating a favorable 0.3% year-over-year change as an approval signal or an unfavorable movement as a conclusion about their own documented capacity.

sections.heading: Turn the trend into documentation questions
body: A practical next step for a West Virginia borrower is to note the latest 4.3% unemployment rate and the 0.3% year-over-year change, then move immediately to the records that describe the household. After noting the 4.3% rate, compare current base pay, variable earnings, self-employment history, contracts, assets, debts, and expected changes with the documentation requirements for the loan path. The 775,073 local labor force and 741,659 employment count can frame the economic conversation, but they should not alter verified borrower inputs. The -0.1% monthly movement remains a population measure even when it appears consistent with a borrower's personal experience. A licensed professional can explain which documents and time periods matter while keeping West Virginia's 4.3% LAUS rate in its proper role as dated local economic context.

articles.methodology: This article uses BLS LAUS state monthly series for labor force, employment, unemployment, and unemployment rate, with prior-month and year-ago comparisons. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: LAUS estimates can be revised, city series are unadjusted, and local labor statistics describe a population rather than an individual applicant's income or employment. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: West Virginia housing costs, ownership, and rent in the latest ACS
articles.dek: Statewide evidence on home value, income, owner costs, housing stock, tenure, vacancy, and rent for borrower planning.
articles.previewText: Read statewide housing-cost, ownership, vacancy, and rent evidence for West Virginia with survey limitations and practical questions.
articles.metaDescription: West Virginia housing costs, ownership, and rent in the latest ACS. Read statewide housing-cost, ownership, vacancy, and rent evidence for West Virginia with.
keyTakeaways: prior ACS state median home value: $119,600.
median monthly owner cost with a mortgage: $1,255.

evidenceFacts.label: prior ACS state median home value
median monthly owner cost with a mortgage
change from the prior ACS home-value estimate

sections.heading: Statewide value, income, and owner costs
body: West Virginia's 2024 ACS housing baseline reports a median owner-occupied home value of $162,600, median household income of $59,608, and median selected monthly owner costs of $1,255 for owners with a mortgage. The $162,600, $59,608, and $1,255 values are separate statewide medians, so they should not be treated as the price, income, and monthly budget of one typical household. The $1,255 measure combines recurring ownership expenses and is not a real-estate-tax figure or a current mortgage quote. For planning, $162,600, $59,608, and $1,255 identify statewide reference points and cost categories, while a real decision still requires local property, borrower, tax, insurance, and loan information.

sections.heading: Keep owner costs and rent comparable
body: The same West Virginia release reports $872 in median gross rent alongside $1,255 in median selected monthly owner costs for owners with a mortgage. The two measures cover different housing populations and cost definitions, so $872 should not be compared with only principal and interest or used to declare a statewide break-even point. A useful comparison replaces the $872 and $1,255 medians with a current lease and a current property budget, including utilities, taxes, insurance, maintenance, association obligations, transaction costs, and the expected time horizon. The $59,608 statewide median can provide economic context, but dividing it by either $872 or $1,255 would still combine medians that do not represent the same household.

sections.heading: How occupied housing is divided by tenure
body: Across West Virginia's measured housing stock of 861,325 units, the owner share of occupied housing is 74.9% and the renter share is 25.1%, making owner-occupied housing the larger statewide tenure group. The 74.9% and 25.1% shares describe how occupied units were distributed during the survey period; they do not measure current buyer demand, owner willingness to sell, or renter readiness to purchase. The 74.9% and 25.1% statewide tenure split is useful for understanding scale and checking assumptions about the housing mix. It remains too broad to select a product or characterize a particular city, county, property type, or household inside West Virginia.

sections.heading: Vacancy is broader than homes for sale
body: West Virginia's vacant-unit share is 15.4% within a statewide stock of 861,325 housing units. ACS vacancy includes categories beyond property currently offered for sale or rent, so 15.4% is not a live inventory rate and cannot measure competition for a selected home. Read beside the 74.9% owner share and 25.1% renter share, the vacancy figure helps describe how the broad stock was classified without predicting near-term supply. Borrowers should use current listings, property condition, days on market, concessions, and local professional context to answer transaction questions that 15.4% and 861,325 were not designed to resolve.

sections.heading: The statewide home-value estimate changed across periods
body: West Virginia's median home-value estimate increased from $119,600 in the prior ACS period to $162,600 in the current release, a reported change of 36.0%. Because both estimates summarize multi-year survey windows, 36.0% is not a point-to-point appreciation rate for a property purchased at $119,600. The 36.0% comparison can show that an old statewide reference may need updating, but it cannot explain the value path in each local market or price segment. Margins of error, changes in the housing mix, and current property evidence should be reviewed before the move from $119,600 to $162,600 influences a purchase, refinance, or equity assumption.

sections.heading: Move from statewide context to a local worksheet
body: A borrower can carry $162,600, $1,255, $872, and the 15.4% vacancy share into a local comparison as dated statewide context, then replace each with evidence matched to the actual decision. For ownership, replace the $162,600 and $1,255 medians with a selected price, down payment, current loan disclosures, taxes, insurance, maintenance, association obligations, and cash reserves. For renting, replace the $872 median with a comparable lease, utilities, deposits, and expected changes over the same horizon. The 25.1% renter share is useful for describing the statewide tenure mix, not for predicting which local option will be available. The 74.9% tenure measure can help explain the statewide housing mix, but it cannot decide whether buying, renting, refinancing, or using equity is suitable for one West Virginia household.

articles.methodology: This article uses 2024 ACS 5-year state estimates for home value, household income, selected owner costs, rent, total and occupied housing units, vacancy, and owner and renter tenure, plus a 2019 value comparison. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: ACS estimates cover multi-year survey periods, carry margins of error, and do not measure current listings, current financing terms, or a specific household's housing costs. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: West Virginia 2026 county loan-limit landscape
articles.dek: How conforming and FHA one-unit limits vary across West Virginia counties and how borrowers can use them responsibly.
articles.previewText: See the range of 2026 conforming and FHA county limits across West Virginia without treating a limit as eligibility or pricing.
articles.metaDescription: West Virginia 2026 county loan-limit landscape. See the range of 2026 conforming and FHA county limits across West Virginia without treating a limit as.
sourceLabels: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
keyTakeaways: counties in the statewide limit summary: 55.

evidenceFacts.label: counties in the statewide limit summary

sections.heading: The conforming range across county records
body: West Virginia's statewide summary covers 55 county or county-equivalent records. Within those records, the 2026 one-unit conforming limit runs from $832,750 to $1,249,125. The $832,750 to $1,249,125 range confirms that one conforming ceiling does not apply uniformly across West Virginia. The 55 record count keeps the range tied to the statewide source coverage rather than an assumed national rule. The $832,750 to $1,249,125 amounts classify loan size within the published program table; they do not establish a borrower's capacity, a property's value, or the price of financing. Before using the $1,249,125 statewide maximum, county, legal unit count, and expected base loan amount must be known.

sections.heading: The FHA range follows its own county table
body: Across the same 55 West Virginia records, the 2026 one-unit FHA limit spans $541,287 to $1,249,125. The $541,287 low and $1,249,125 high show that FHA's published boundary also varies within the state. The $541,287 floor and $1,249,125 high remain county-table values even when they match conforming amounts elsewhere. The FHA range should not be merged with the $832,750 to $1,249,125 conforming range, because the programs use different authorities and requirements. A $541,287 to $1,249,125 comparison can route loan-amount and product-category questions, but it cannot determine FHA eligibility, mortgage insurance, appraisal acceptability, occupancy compliance, cash needs, or approval.

sections.heading: Why county verification matters
body: 1 West Virginia counties sit above the conforming baseline in this summary, while the full conforming range reaches from $832,750 to $1,249,125. That count shows where a statewide shortcut can fail: a property in a higher-limit county may use a different published ceiling than one at $832,750. The 1 figure does not mean those counties are more affordable, more desirable, or more likely to approve a loan. It only describes how many matched county rows exceed the baseline. The $1,249,125 maximum should therefore be treated as evidence that county lookup matters, not as the default West Virginia limit. Before treating $1,249,125 as relevant, a borrower should identify the property's official county or county-equivalent area and then attach conventional, jumbo, or FHA labels to the desired loan amount.

sections.heading: Conforming and FHA ranges answer different questions
body: The highest one-unit conforming amount in West Virginia is $1,249,125, while the highest FHA amount is $1,249,125; at the low end, the figures are $832,750 and $541,287. These pairs show program boundaries, not competing offers. A requested amount below $1,249,125 may still fall outside the applicable county's conforming limit, and an amount below $1,249,125 may still face FHA borrower and property requirements. Comparing $832,750, $1,249,125, $541,287, and $1,249,125 is useful only when county and unit count are held constant. The $1,249,125 and $1,249,125 ceilings leave rates, fees, mortgage insurance, appraisal, reserves, debt capacity, and product suitability as separate questions for the documented transaction.

sections.heading: A larger county limit is not greater affordability
body: A $1,249,125 conforming ceiling or $1,249,125 FHA ceiling does not make that loan amount affordable to a West Virginia household, and the lower $832,750 or $541,287 figures do not describe home prices. Published $1,249,125 and $1,249,125 limits cannot establish borrower income, debts, credit, assets, occupancy, property condition, appraisal, insurance, reserves, rates, fees, or final approval. The $1,249,125 and $1,249,125 figures can also change with a new calendar year or corrected property geography. Keeping the 55 county-record scope and 2026 period attached to the figures prevents the statewide summary from being mistaken for a personalized product recommendation or a single amount that applies everywhere.

sections.heading: Use the range to reach the correct county row
body: The next step is to verify the selected property's West Virginia county or county-equivalent area, legal unit count, price, down-payment plan, and expected base loan amount. Then compare that amount with the exact official row, using the $832,750 to $1,249,125 conforming range and $541,287 to $1,249,125 FHA range only as orientation. The 1 above-baseline count explains why this lookup matters, but it does not replace it. Keep the selected county row with the worksheet so the $1,249,125 and $1,249,125 statewide maximums cannot be mistaken for property-specific figures. After the 1 count prompts that county lookup, a licensed professional can explain current limits and borrower, property, mortgage-insurance, and documentation rules without presenting the largest statewide figure as available borrowing power.

articles.methodology: This article joins official 2026 FHFA conforming and HUD FHA county files by five-digit county FIPS, then summarizes the observed one-unit range and county counts for the state. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: State summaries can conceal special exceptions and multi-unit differences, and official program updates or property-location corrections can change the applicable row. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.
